_**User (2026-03-19 02:00:10Z)**_

df.head() 

How do I view the full cell / first item again? 

(How to do indexing)

---

_**Agent (copilot/gpt-5.3-codex)**_

Perfect question. In pandas:

- df.head() shows the first 5 rows.
- To see only the first row:
  df.iloc[0]
- To keep it as a 1-row table (instead of a Series):
  df.iloc[[0]]

If your text is getting cut off/truncated, enable full display first:

pd.set_option("display.max_colwidth", None)
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", None)
pd.set_option("display.width", 0)

Then run:

df.iloc[[0]]

If you want the first value from a specific column (for example column named prompt):

df.loc[0, "prompt"]

Quick indexing cheat sheet:

- First row: df.iloc[0]
- First row, first column: df.iloc[0, 0]
- First 3 rows: df.iloc[:3]
- Column by name: df["prompt"]
- First row of that column: df["prompt"].iloc[0]

If you paste your column names, I can give you the exact one-liner for “first full item” in your dataset.

---

_**User (2026-03-19 02:01:55Z)**_

How do I present my rows s.t I don't see the \n

---

_**Agent (copilot/gpt-5.3-codex)**_

You have two different cases:

1. Real newline characters
2. Literal backslash-n text

Use this to handle both and show a clean row:

~~~python
df_clean = df.replace({r"\\n": " ", r"\n": " "}, regex=True)
df_clean.iloc[[0]]
~~~

If you want to view one field with proper line breaks (instead of seeing \n), use print:

~~~python
text = df.loc[0, "prompt"]   # change "prompt" to your column name
print(text.replace("\\n", "\n"))
~~~

Quick rule:
- For table display without \n: use df_clean
- For readable multiline text: use print(...)
